I have been to Italy three times.
Have you ever been to a vineyard?
I have never taken a cruise.
She has seen twenty-three cities in this country.
I have been to Paris.
Have you ever eaten in a square at midnight?
I went to Paris in 2022.
We ate in that square last Tuesday.
be, was, been. See, saw, seen. Take, took, taken. Eat, ate, eaten.
I have never been to Ireland, but I have always wanted to see the cliffs.
Is this seat free? I am Declan. I came on the bus from Florence with the others. Have you ever been to a vineyard before?
Please, sit. No, I have never been to a vineyard. This is the first one in my life. I have tried a lot of Italian wine, but I have never seen where it comes from.
Neither have I. This is my first one too. I am from Cork, in Ireland. We have no vineyards there. We have rain, and we have cows. Have you ever been to Ireland?
No, I have never been to Ireland. But I have always wanted to see the cliffs on the coast, the big ones. Is it true that you can walk on the top?
The Cliffs of Moher. You can, and you should. It is worth it, and the wind takes your hat. And you? Have you been to Italy before?
Three times. I have seen twenty-three cities in this country. I have walked the coast at Positano, and I have been to Capri. It is an island, and the cliffs go straight down into the water.
Twenty-three! I have only been to Rome. I have never seen the south, and I have never taken one of the small trains. Which one do I have to see, if I have one week?
The south. Go to the coast, and go to the small towns in the countryside, not only the landmarks. And take the train. I have never taken a cruise, but I have taken almost every train in this country.
That is the best answer I have had all week. My wife asked me the same question last month, and I did not have one word for her.
Then tell her a woman from Sao Paulo said it: go south. And eat your ice cream in a square, at midnight.
Yes, I have. I have been to Italy three times.
No, I have never been, but I have always wanted to.
I have walked the coast at Positano, and I have seen Capri.
I went there in 2016, and it was the best trip of my life.
And you? Have you ever been to Brazil?

Good afternoon, everybody, and welcome. My name is Matteo, and my family has this vineyard. Before we drink anything, three minutes of my voice, and then the wine, I promise. First: where you are. You are standing on a hill, and everything you see, all the way down to that road, is grapes. My grandfather planted the first line in 1953. He never left this hill. He never went abroad, not one time in his life. He has been to Florence twice, and Florence is fifty kilometers away, and he called that a long trip. Second: what you are going to taste. Three wines, all from this countryside, all from this hill. And here is the thing I want you to understand. People ask me: Matteo, have you traveled? And I say no. I have never taken a cruise. I have never seen the coast of Ireland. I have never been on a plane. I am fifty-eight years old and I have never been on a plane, and everybody at this table has, and today you are all here, on my hill. So who has traveled? Third, and then we drink. In this room today there are people from six countries. I have shown this vineyard to people from more than forty countries in my life, and I have learned one thing from all of them. Everybody asks the same first question. Have you ever been to my country? And I always say no. And then they tell me about it. The square in their city. The castle. The island. The food their mother makes. And for three minutes I have been there. That is my traveling. Forty countries, and I have never left this hill. Now. The glasses are in front of you. Welcome.

Hello. My name is Margaret, I am sixty-nine years old, and I want to talk to you about one question. Just one. Wherever you go in this world, when you sit next to somebody, on a train, at a table, in a line at a museum, sooner or later that person is going to ask you this: have you ever been to Greece? Have you ever tried this? Have you ever seen that? It is the first question. It is always the first question. And I want to tell you what I did with it for about thirty years. I said yes. Or I said no. One word. That is all. And not because I did not have the story. I had the story! I have been to nineteen countries. I have seen the northern lights. I have eaten things I cannot pronounce. I have slept in a castle in Scotland with no heating, and I have never been so cold in my life. The stories were all in here. But somebody said, have you ever been to Norway, and I said yes. Yes. One word. And then silence. And then that person turned to somebody else, and the conversation went away from me, and I sat there with nineteen countries in my head and a cup of tea in my hand, and nobody knew. Nobody knew. That is the part that hurts, and that is why I am telling you. Now listen, because this is the whole thing, and it is small. When somebody asks you, have you ever, they are not asking for yes or no. They are opening a door. So do not answer with a word. Answer with a sentence. Yes, I have. I went in 2019, and it was the coldest week of my life. Or: no, I have never been, but I have always wanted to. That is it. That is the difference between yes, and a friend. And you do not need perfect English for it. You need the sentence. So next time, on a hill, at a table, with a stranger, do not say yes. Say what happened.
